name: Visconti-Sforza Tarot — The Oldest Near-Complete Tarot (attr. Bonifacio Bembo, c. 1451)
description:
# Visconti-Sforza Tarot

> For *why* tarot was invented at all — the imported card game, the "fifth suit" of
> triumphs, and whether any divination tradition predates it — see the deep-dive
> `00b-origins-and-predecessors.mdx`.

## At a glance

The oldest **near-complete** tarot we have: a luxury, hand-painted deck made for the
ruling house of Milan around 1451. It is a **game** deck — the cards were still called
*trionfi*, not "tarot," and certainly not used for divination. Its trump sequence is the
headwater of the **C-order** branch that flows down to the Tarot de Marseille and, much
later, the occult decks.

## Origin & dating

"Visconti-Sforza" is a collective name for roughly **fifteen** related, incomplete sets
from the mid-15th century. The best-known (the Pierpont-Morgan-Bergamo deck, c.1451) was
commissioned around the marriage-alliance world of **Filippo Maria Visconti**, Duke of
Milan, and his successor and son-in-law **Francesco Sforza**. Much of the painting is
attributed to **Bonifacio Bembo** (or his workshop), an early-Renaissance Milanese
miniaturist. Materials are unambiguously elite: tempera, tooled gold leaf, heraldic
devices of the two dynasties.

## Provenance & evidence

The cards are dispersed across the Morgan Library (New York), the Accademia Carrara and
Colleoni collection (Bergamo), and elsewhere — hence the alternate name
*Colleoni-Baglioni*. Several decks are missing trumps (one later set has only the
Emperor and the Wheel surviving). The heraldry and the documented Visconti/Sforza
patronage anchor the dating better than for almost any other early deck.

## Game or divination?

**Game, unambiguously.** No divinatory apparatus, no fortune-telling text, no
cartomantic tradition attaches to these objects in their own century. They were played
as a trick-taking game by the Milanese court. Any "meaning" beyond the iconography is a
later overlay imported in the 18th–19th centuries.

## The fear question

**No fear.** This is conspicuous *consumption*, not clandestine ritual — a duke's
heraldry is literally painted on the cards. Playing was a marker of status. The only
contemporary anxiety around cards was the friars' campaign against **gambling** (see
Bernardino of Siena), and even that targeted the wager, not the imagery. A 1451 Milanese
courtier would have shown these off, not hidden them.

## Counter-voices

The "secret Egyptian/Hermetic deck" reading has been retro-fitted onto Visconti imagery
by later occultists, but nothing in the patron context, materials, or use supports it.
The figures are standard Italian Christian-allegorical types (Pope, Popess, the Virtues,
Death, the Wheel of Fortune) — courtly and Catholic, not crypto-pagan.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Exact internal dating and workshop attribution of each of the ~15 sets is still
  debated; "c.1451" is a convenience label for the Morgan-Bergamo deck.
- The original trump *numbering* was not printed on the cards; reconstructed order
  depends on later C-order witnesses.

## Sources & further reading

- "Visconti-Sforza Tarot," Wikipedia; Morgan Library & Museum collection notes.
- "From Milan to New York: The Adventures of the Visconti-Sforza Tarot Deck," Tarot Heritage.
- Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980).

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** Surviving cards split across **Morgan Library (≈35), Accademia Carrara, Bergamo (≈26), and Casa Colleoni (≈13)**. The six later replacement cards are Fortitude, Temperance, Star, Moon, Sun, World — their attribution to **Antonio Cicognara** is **discredited** (it rests on a 19th-c. forgery); treat any 'second hand = Cicognara' note as superseded. Bembo vs the Zavattari workshop remains a live attribution debate. See [research/decks/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/decks/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).

sections.History: Unnumbered and unnamed in the original game — the wild card that stands outside the sequence of triumphs. It survives in the Morgan Library portion of the deck.
sections.Reading: The zero before counting begins: the holy fool, innocence and folly walking together at the edge of the procession.
sections.Iconography: A ragged man in a tattered tunic, bare-legged, seven feathers standing in his hair and a club over his shoulder. He is the village madman, the social outcast painted with the same gold leaf as kings.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
A ragged man in a tattered tunic, bare-legged, with feathers standing in his hair and a club over his shoulder — the village madman, painted in the same gold as kings. In Moakley's reading he stands outside the count as the personification of **Lent** answering the Carnival King [@moakley1966; @web_moakley_magus]. Survives (Morgan portion). 

*Sources:* [@moakley1966], [@web_morgan_vs_ms], [@web_wopc_vs1460].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).
sections.Scene: A ragged youth strides forward with a staff over his shoulder; feathers or leaves are tucked into his hair and he wears tattered clothing. His gaze is upward and his posture suggests purposeful wandering.
sections.Symbol: The Fool in the VS deck is not a clown but a wandering figure outside the social hierarchy — neither noble nor peasant by appearance, carrying everything on his back. Feathers signal a person outside convention. The absence of a number confirms his liminal position: he does not belong to the sequence of trumps but moves among them. In 15th-century Italian court culture he embodied the figure licensed to speak uncomfortable truths.

sections.History: "Bagatella" means a trifle; he is the lowest of the triumphs, the everyman who opens the parade. The earliest, most worldly figure of the trumps.
sections.Reading: Human skill and craft, the hand at work — beginnings rooted in the ordinary and the makeshift.
sections.Iconography: A young man seated at a table set with small objects, a wand or knife in hand — a market conjuror, cobbler, or sleight-of-hand trickster.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.

sections.Scene: A bearded man in a red and green pointed hat sits at a workbench or table covered with small objects; he appears to be demonstrating or manipulating the items laid before him.
sections.Symbol: Il Bagatto (the juggler, prestidigitator, or mountebank) occupies the lowest ranked trump — just above the unranked Fool. His table of objects shows the raw materials of the minor suits: coins, cups, and implements. In court allegory he represents earthly skill and manual ingenuity, the lowest of the cosmic forces in the trump hierarchy derived from Petrarch's *Trionfi*. His pointed hat is a type worn by conjurers and marginalized figures.

sections.History: The most scandalous card in the deck: she is almost certainly Sister Maifreda da Pirovano, a Visconti kinswoman elected 'popess' by the heretical Guglielmite sect and burned at the stake in 1300. A buried family memory rendered in gold.
sections.Reading: Hidden wisdom and forbidden authority — the feminine that holds a sacred book the world would rather she did not open.
sections.Iconography: A crowned woman in the brown habit of a nun, a triple papal tiara on her head and a book in her lap.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
A crowned woman in the **brown habit of a nun** (with a knotted cord belt), a triple papal tiara on her head and a book in her lap. She is widely identified as **Sister Maifreda da Pirovano**, a Visconti kinswoman elected "popess" by the heretical **Guglielmite** sect and burned at the stake in Milan in **1300** [@web_th_vs_popess; @web_greer_maifreda]. 

*Sources:* [@moakley1966], [@web_greer_maifreda], [@web_th_vs_popess].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).
sections.Scene: A crowned woman in papal robes is enthroned; she holds a closed book in one hand and a tall papal staff in the other. Her expression is composed and authoritative.
sections.Symbol: The Papessa (female pope) is one of the most debated images in tarot history — no other early European card game included a female pope. The VS figure likely references a concept of feminine spiritual authority rather than any specific person. Her attributes — the book (scripture or wisdom), the staff, the papal tiara — are all male pontifical symbols transferred to a female body, signaling an exceptional or allegorical status rather than institutional office.

sections.History: Worldly feminine sovereignty wedded to the dynasty's heraldry — the Empress is the house made flesh.
sections.Reading: Abundance, fertile authority, the generative power that rules from a throne rather than a garden.
sections.Iconography: A crowned, enthroned woman bearing a shield emblazoned with the imperial eagle of the Visconti.

sections.Scene: A standing woman in gold brocade holds a heraldic shield bearing an eagle and wears blue angel wings; she faces forward with a commanding expression.
sections.Symbol: The Empress in the VS deck bears the imperial eagle — the emblem of the Holy Roman Empire — marking her as a cosmic counterpart to earthly imperial authority. Her wings distinguish her from a simple portrait of an empress: she is an allegorical figure of imperial feminine power, possibly linked to the Virgin Mary's iconographic tradition of the *Woman Clothed with the Sun*. Gold brocade is the textile of supreme courtly rank.

sections.Reading: Structure, command, the steadying weight of the throne and the law it enforces.
sections.Iconography: A bearded sovereign enthroned, the imperial eagle on his shield, an orb or sceptre in hand.

sections.Scene: An elderly bearded man in blue robes is seated on a throne; he gestures with one hand and a small black eagle perches on his hat.
sections.Symbol: The Emperor's small black eagle on his hat is the imperial device of the Holy Roman Empire. His seated, gesturing posture is the conventional iconography of sovereign authority — commanding rather than acting. The contrast with the Empress (who stands and holds the shield) suggests the VS programme distinguishes active emblematic authority from seated judicial power.

sections.Reading: Tradition, blessing, the institutional channel through which the sacred is made official.
sections.Iconography: A pope in the triple tiara, hand raised in blessing, the crossed keys of Saint Peter near him.

sections.Scene: A bearded pope in white and blue vestments is enthroned; he raises his right hand in a blessing gesture and holds a tall papal staff or triregnum.
sections.Symbol: The Pope is the ecclesiastical counterpart to the Emperor — the two highest worldly authorities in medieval Christian political theory, temporal and spiritual. His blessing gesture and triple-crowned staff are the conventional attributes of pontifical authority. Placed adjacent to the Emperor in the trump hierarchy, the VS deck follows the two-swords doctrine of medieval political theology.

sections.Reading: Union and choice, the binding of two houses — desire pressed into the service of lineage.

sections.Scene: A winged nude Cupid is suspended in midair above; below, a young man in courtly dress stands between two women, one of whom reaches toward him or crowns him.
sections.Symbol: The Love card shows the *trionfo d'amore* — the Triumph of Love — drawn directly from Petrarch's *Trionfi*. Cupid above shoots or presides over the scene; the youth below is caught between two feminine figures, one often interpreted as secular love and the other as virtue or matrimony. The VS version frames this as a courtly betrothal scene — appropriate for a deck likely associated with the Visconti-Sforza dynastic alliances.

sections.History: The card that names the whole game: a Roman-style 'trionfo', the victory parade from which 'trumps' descend.
sections.Reading: Victory and momentum, the self carried forward in triumph over what it has mastered.
sections.Iconography: A crowned lady stands in a triumphal car, sometimes drawn by winged horses, holding orb and sceptre.

sections.Scene: A young noblewoman in elaborate dress rides in an open chariot drawn by two white horses; her posture is upright and dignified.
sections.Symbol: The Chariot in Italian early tarot is often a *trionfo* procession float — the chariot of Roman military triumph adapted for courtly allegory. The VS charioteer is female, distinguishing her from the male military triumphs of ancient Rome. Two white horses signal purity and control. The open chariot emphasizes public spectacle: this is a figure who conquers through presence rather than combat.

sections.History: In this pre-occult ordering Justice sits at VIII (the Golden Dawn would later swap it with Strength). The mounted knight is a Visconti flourish.
sections.Reading: Balance and reckoning, the blade and the scale — measure applied without flinching.
sections.Iconography: An enthroned woman holds an upraised sword and a balance; above her a small armoured knight rides across the gold.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
An enthroned woman holding an upraised **sword** and a **balance**; above her a small armoured **knight rides** across the gold — a Visconti flourish. In this pre-occult C-order Justice sits at **VIII** (the Golden Dawn would much later swap it with Strength). 

*Sources:* [@moakley1966], [@web_wopc_vs1460].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).
sections.Scene: A woman in grey-blue holds two swords upright; in the background a mounted knight appears.
sections.Symbol: Justice holds two swords rather than the conventional scales-and-single-sword, making this an unusual VS variant. Two swords may reference the two-swords doctrine (temporal and spiritual authority) or simply emphasize the double force of justice. The mounted knight in the background, rare in tarot iconography, grounds the allegorical figure in the reality of military enforcement — justice backed by force.

sections.History: Originally 'Il Tempo' — Time, or the Old Man — not yet the lamp-bearing Hermit of later decks. The hourglass marks his true subject.
sections.Reading: Time, age, and solitary measure — the slow accounting of hours that no triumph escapes.
sections.Iconography: A stooped old man with a long beard holds aloft not a lantern but an hourglass, wrapped in heavy robes.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
A stooped, long-bearded old man holding aloft not a lantern but an **hourglass**, wrapped in heavy robes. Originally **Time (Il Tempo)** — the Old Man — not yet the lamp-bearing Hermit of later decks. 

*Sources:* [@moakley1966], [@web_moakley_magus].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).
sections.Scene: An elderly man in blue robes and a wide-brimmed hat walks with a tall staff; he carries an hourglass in his other hand.
sections.Symbol: The Old Man (later called the Hermit) holds an hourglass rather than a lantern — making time the central symbol rather than guidance or wisdom. The hourglass explicitly connects this figure to Saturn and to Petrarch's *Trionfo del Tempo* (Triumph of Time). In the early trump sequence, this figure often represented Time itself consuming all earthly fame. The wide hat and staff suggest the pilgrim or wanderer archetype, but the hourglass anchors the allegory to temporality.

illustrations.scene: Wheel of Fortune (La Ruota della Fortuna)

sections.History: A medieval commonplace given Bembo's gold — 'regnabo, regno, regnavi, sum sine regno' (I shall reign, I reign, I have reigned, I am without reign).
sections.Reading: The turning of fortune, rise and fall — the one law that levels emperor and fool alike.
sections.Iconography: A blindfolded Fortuna turns a great wheel; four figures cling to it, some sprouting asses' ears and tails as they rise and fall, mottoes streaming from their mouths.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
A blindfolded **Fortuna** turns a great wheel; four figures cling to it, some sprouting asses' ears and tails as they rise and fall, mottoes streaming from their mouths — the medieval *regnabo / regno / regnavi / sum sine regno* ("I shall reign / I reign / I have reigned / I am without reign"). 

*Sources:* [@moakley1966], [@web_wopc_vs1460].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).
sections.Scene: A blindfolded woman turns a large golden wheel; human figures cling to the wheel at its four positions — rising on the left, at the top, descending on the right, at the bottom.
sections.Symbol: Fortune's Wheel is one of the oldest medieval allegories, inherited from Boethius's *Consolation of Philosophy* (6th century). The blindfolded Fortune cannot see whom she raises or lowers — her actions are indifferent to merit. The four figures on the wheel represent the arc of earthly ambition: *regno* (I reign), *regnavi* (I reigned), *sum sine regno* (I am without reign), *regnabo* (I shall reign). In the VS deck the wheel is golden, emphasizing the stakes of courtly fortune in a period when the Sforza had just seized power.

sections.History: One of the six cards repainted by a later hand (c.1480s–90s, often attributed to Antonio Cicognara) after the Bembo original was lost. The style is visibly different from the first artist's.
sections.Reading: Fortitude — courage that masters the beast not by force alone but by steady will.
sections.Iconography: A standing figure subdues a lion, gripping it in a Hercules-like display of mastery.

sections.Scene: A youth in a landscape strikes a rearing lion with a club, subduing it through physical force.
sections.Symbol: Fortitude (Strength) in the VS deck uses the Herculean model: the hero who defeats the lion by brute strength. This is the Hercules-and-Nemean-Lion type rather than the later RWS version of gentle spiritual dominion. In the context of the Sforza commission, Fortitude as physical prowess referenced Francesco Sforza's identity as a condottiere — a soldier who won Milan through military strength. The lion is the archetypal emblem of courage, here being mastered by a force even greater.

sections.History: A 'pittura infamante' — the shaming portrait Italian cities painted of traitors and debtors, hung by the foot. Only later did occultists read it as sacrifice.
sections.Reading: Suspension and reversal — the inverted view, the betrayer's punishment, the world seen from upside down.
sections.Iconography: A youth hangs upside down by one ankle from a wooden frame, hands bound behind his back, the free leg crossed.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Research note:
A youth hangs **upside down by one ankle** from a wooden frame, hands bound behind his back, the free leg crossed — a ***pittura infamante***, the shaming portrait Italian cities painted of traitors and debtors. The sacrificial reading is a much later occult overlay. 

*Sources:* [@moakley1966], [@web_wopc_vs1460].

*Full card-by-card research:* [research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md](https://github.com/PlayfulProcess/recursive-tarot/blob/dev/research/cards/visconti-sforza-tarot.md).
sections.Scene: A figure in a white tunic and green tights hangs upside down by one ankle from a frame; the composition is vertical and the posture is calm despite the inversion.
sections.Symbol: The Hanged Man (*il traditore*, the traitor) references the public punishment of hanging traitors upside down by one foot — a form of public shaming used in 15th-century Italy. The VS figure's calm expression and fine clothing suggest an allegory of voluntary sacrifice or spiritual inversion rather than criminal punishment. The green tights and white tunic give him courtly dignity. Inversion in iconographic tradition signals the reversal of worldly values: what appears as defeat from below is victory from above.

sections.Scene: A white skeleton stands frontally, facing the viewer, and holds a bow and arrow; the figure is set against a plain gold background.
sections.Symbol: Death in the VS deck is strikingly frontal and confrontational — unlike later TdM versions where Death rides a horse or sweeps with a scythe. The bow and arrow weapon is unusual: it connects Death to Cupid (also depicted with bow and arrow in the Love card), suggesting a structural rhyme between love and death in the trump sequence. The plain gold background gives Death an iconic, almost sacred quality — death as absolute rather than narrative.
sections.Iconographic lineage: The "great equaliser of the *danse macabre*" framing here and the courtly *Triumph of Death* (see the Cary-Yale Death) are two streams of a single idea, not opposing ones: the popular Dance of Death and Petrarch's learned Triumph both teach that Death spares no estate. The Visconti-Sforza figure stands at that confluence — a court object carrying a message the whole culture, high and low, already shared.

sections.History: Another of the six later-hand restorations. Temperance's gesture — mixing without spilling — is among the oldest stable tarot images.
sections.Reading: Tempering and flow — the patient blending of waters, moderation as a living art.
sections.Iconography: A winged woman pours liquid between two vessels in an unbroken stream.

sections.Scene: A young woman in a blue star-spangled gown pours liquid between two ornate vessels in a careful, steady gesture.
sections.Symbol: Temperance (from Latin *temperare*, to mix in due proportion) pours between two vessels — the classical iconography of mixing water with wine to the right proportion, avoiding excess. Her star-spangled gown is uncommon and may reflect Visconti heraldic devices or the celestial sphere. Temperance is one of the four Cardinal Virtues (with Justice, Fortitude, and Prudence) that anchor the moral architecture of the major arcana. The steady gesture and careful posture enact the virtue itself.

sections.History: One of four cards never recovered from this deck. Whether it was lost, destroyed, or — as some suggest — deliberately removed, we cannot know. Its absence is part of the deck's history.
sections.Reading: Bondage, appetite, the shadow side of power — read here through a deliberate, instructive silence.
sections.Iconography: No Bembo card survives. Later decks in the tradition show a horned, clawed figure presiding over bound captives; the Visconti-Sforza original is simply gone.

sections.Scene: Card not extant in the surviving Visconti-Sforza deck. Based on the standard 15th-century Italian trump programme, the Devil would have depicted a demonic figure — horned, winged, and dark — presiding over chained figures representing the enslaved passions or spiritual captivity.
sections.Symbol: The Devil is absent from the physical VS cards that survive (the Pierpont Morgan Bergamo deck is missing several cards). In the trump hierarchy the Devil represents the force below Death and above the Tower — the power of evil in the cosmic order before the redemptive catastrophe. Its absence from the VS is historically significant: the two lost 'dark' trumps (Devil and Tower) are precisely those with the most uncomfortable theological implications for an aristocratic patron.

sections.Scene: Card not extant in the surviving Visconti-Sforza deck. Based on the standard 15th-century Italian trump programme, the Tower would have depicted a tall structure being struck by lightning or fire, with figures falling from it.
sections.Symbol: The Tower (*la torre*, also called the House of God or the Fire from Heaven) is likewise missing from the surviving VS cards. It represents sudden catastrophic destruction — the fall of worldly pride. Its iconographic ancestor is the Tower of Babel and the lightning bolt of divine judgment. The absence of both the Devil and Tower from the surviving VS deck has led some scholars to speculate they were deliberately excluded from the original commission, though most attribute the gap to physical loss over 500 years.

sections.History: One of the six later restorations. The Visconti Star is a simple votive figure, far from the kneeling water-pourer of the Marseille and Waite decks.
sections.Reading: Hope and orientation — a fixed light to steer by after the dark of Death and the lost Tower.
sections.Iconography: A woman stands holding aloft a single great eight-pointed star against the gold.

sections.Scene: A standing woman in blue and red robes holds a large gold star aloft in one hand; her posture is upright and ceremonial.
sections.Symbol: The Star in the VS deck is held rather than depicted in the sky — the woman presents the star as an object or emblem. This distinguishes it from later iconography where a nude figure kneels below streaming stars. The gold star is likely a Visconti heraldic symbol — the Visconti used star devices in their emblems. As a trump, the Star follows the catastrophe of the Tower (if it were present) and signals hope, guidance, or celestial constancy after destruction.

sections.Symbol: The Moon is personified as a woman carrying the crescent — the conventional classical iconography of Luna or Diana. The ribbon or rope in her other hand is unusual and may reference a tidal pull or the moon's influence over earthly rhythms. In the hierarchy of cosmic forces, the Moon precedes the Sun and the Star — the natural celestial order ascending from earthly to divine. The VS Moon is a dignified personification rather than the dream-landscape of later decks.

sections.Scene: Two trumpeting angels flank a central divine figure at the top; below, two human figures rise from a stone sarcophagus, their hands raised.
sections.Symbol: The Last Judgment (*il Giudizio*) depicts the Christian resurrection of the dead — the dead rising from the grave at the sound of the angel's trump. This is the penultimate trump before the World, making the cosmic sequence end with divine judgment and then eternal order. The two rising figures are the resurrected saved; the angels' trumpets are the signal that ends historical time. The VS version compresses the standard iconography into a simple vertical composition: heaven above, resurrection below.

sections.History: The last and highest triumph, and the final card of the six restorations — the triumph that overcomes all other triumphs.
sections.Reading: Completion and wholeness — the cosmos held up to be seen, the journey of the trionfi closed.
sections.Iconography: Two winged putti hold up a great orb in which a walled city floats above water and hills, crowned by a star or a standing figure.

sections.Scene: Two winged putti hold a large circular globe between them; within the globe a walled city or castle is depicted in miniature.
sections.Symbol: The World (*il Mondo*) in the VS deck is a globe held by two putti — unlike later versions featuring a dancing figure within a wreath. The globe enclosing a walled city refers to the *orbis terrarum* (the circle of the world) of medieval cosmography, within which human civilization exists. Two putti holding the world sphere echo the iconography of divine Providence — the world is upheld by celestial agents. This is the highest trump, representing the perfected order of creation.

sections.History: A pip card of Coins (Denari), the suit of Earth. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Ace of Coins — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Iconography: Ace of Coins: a single coin arranged amid gold-leaf scrollwork and Visconti-Sforza heraldic flourishes on a punched gold ground. Like all early Italian pips, it is decorative rather than scenic — there is no narrative picture, only the suit-emblems themselves made splendid.

sections.Scene: A single large coin bearing a heraldic coat of arms is displayed at centre; botanical vines with flowers and foliage fill the card border.
sections.Symbol: The Ace presents the suit emblem in its purest and most dignified form — a single object without number or arrangement. The heraldic arms on the VS coin embed dynastic identity into the suit itself: this is not an abstract coin but the coin of the Visconti-Sforza court. Botanical borders throughout the VS minors signal courtly abundance — the garden as a space of aristocratic pleasure and cultivated nature, distinct from the wild.

sections.Scene: Two coins are arranged vertically at centre; a ribbon banner bearing the text 'AIEN DROYE' (a variant of the Visconti motto 'a bon droyt') crosses the card horizontally.
sections.Symbol: From Two onward the VS minor pips introduce the Visconti-Sforza motto — 'a bon droyt' (with good right), asserting legitimate authority over every card in the deck. Even the minor pips carry a dynastic claim. Two coins arranged vertically is the simplest compositional expansion from the Ace: number is introduced, but the objects remain isolated in botanical space without narrative tension.

sections.Symbol: Three objects introduce the first spatial problem of pip composition: they can be arranged in a line, triangle, or offset stack. The VS choice is typically triangular — a stable geometric form that resolves the odd number. The botanical border and motto banners remain the organizing visual framework, asserting that even at Three the cards are heraldic declarations rather than narrative scenes.

sections.Scene: Four coins are arranged in a two-by-two grid; a banner carries the Visconti motto; botanical border fills the card.
sections.Symbol: Four resolves naturally into a two-by-two square — the most stable geometric arrangement, reflecting the medieval ordering principle of quaternity (four elements, four humors, four cardinal virtues). The VS four of coins achieves visual equilibrium through symmetry, with the motto banner providing horizontal punctuation.

sections.Scene: Five coins are arranged in a quincunx pattern — two above, one at centre, two below; two banner inscriptions carry the motto; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Five disrupts the four-square stability with a central coin that asserts itself above the balanced pair below. The quincunx (five-spot dice pattern) is the oldest classical arrangement for five objects and appears in Roman mosaics and architectural ornament. The central coin in the quincunx can be read as a pivot or focal point that animates an otherwise static arrangement.

In numerological tradition, six is the number of perfection (the sum of its divisors) and of the days of creation. The simple grid emphasizes repetition and abundance rather than hierarchy.

sections.Symbol: Seven is the most compositionally challenging single-digit number for pip design because it cannot resolve into a perfect square or rectangle. The VS seven of coins uses an irregular arrangement that disrupts the regularity of the earlier cards. Seven is the number most associated with divine completion in Christian numerology (seven days, seven sacraments, seven virtues), but visually it introduces imbalance.

sections.Symbol: Eight returns to perfect symmetry as a two-by-four arrangement. Eight is the number of renewal and resurrection in Christian symbolism (the octagon appears in baptisteries for this reason), and the octagonal thrones on which several VS court figures are seated echo this iconographic use. The VS eight of coins is visually the most regular of the higher pip cards.

sections.Symbol: Nine fills a three-by-three square — the maximum regular grid before Ten disrupts the pattern again. Three-times-three has deep symbolic resonance in Christian Trinitarian thought (Trinity of Trinities), and the three-by-three arrangement appears across medieval sacred geometry. The VS nine of coins is abundant and ordered.

sections.Scene: Ten coins are arranged in a two-by-five configuration; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Ten, the complete number, cannot form a perfect square without remainder. The VS ten of coins resolves this with a two-by-five arrangement — abundance expressed as column pairs. Ten in medieval numerology represents completion and return to unity (1+0), the full cycle of number. The VS ten of coins card is the most densely populated coin card, signaling the culmination of the suit's material abundance.

sections.History: A court card of Coins (Denari). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Page of Coins — the student and messenger of the suit — its energy young, curious, not yet committed, expressed through the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Iconography: Page of Coins: a courtly fante figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the coin of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.

sections.Scene: A youth in an ornate blue and gold cloak stands on a grass ground, looking upward and to the right with an open, wondering expression.
sections.Symbol: The Page (or Knave, *fante*) is the youngest court figure — a youth in training for courtly service. The upward gaze suggests curiosity, aspiration, or receptivity: the Page is learning to look toward higher things. The ornate cloak indicates a noble household position rather than a common servant. In Italian court tarot, Pages represent the entry point into the suit's sphere of action — for Coins, the beginning of engagement with material value and stewardship.

sections.History: The Knight of Coins is one of the four cards lost from the Morgan-Bergamo deck — no Morgan-Bergamo example survives. The image shown here is the Knight of Coins from the Cary-Yale sister deck (c. 1442, same Bembo workshop), where it does survive, standing in for the lost original.
sections.Reading: Knight of Coins — the suit in motion — questing, active, riding out to meet its element, expressed through the material world — money, body, land, and the slow work of provision.
sections.Iconography: Knight of Coins: a courtly cavallo figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the coin of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.

sections.Scene: A rider in a pink and yellow tunic sits on a white horse against a gold-tooled background; this is a larger-format Cary-Yale card.
sections.Symbol: The Knight (*cavaliere*) is the active, mounted court figure — military readiness personified. The white horse signals purity and aristocratic breeding. The gold-tooled background places this card in the most luxurious stratum of the VS commission; the Cary-Yale format (slightly larger and more ornate) suggests this card may have been painted for a different portion of the commission than the smaller Bergamo cards. The Knight of Coins is associated with the stewardship of material resources in active, outward-facing service.

sections.Scene: A woman in a black and gold patterned gown is seated in profile; a large coin rests on her lap.
sections.Symbol: The Queen of Coins is the most materially grounded female court figure. Her profile posture — shared with several VS queens — follows the conventions of Italian court portraiture (profile was the prestige format for medals and frescoes). The large coin on her lap is her emblem of authority: she holds the material world in her lap, governs it through possession rather than action. Black and gold brocade is among the most expensive fabric combinations in 15th-century court dress.

sections.Scene: A young man in a dark patterned tunic sits on an octagonal throne; he holds a staff in one hand and a coin in the other; his legs are in parti-colored hose.
sections.Symbol: The King of Coins holds both the coin (emblematic authority over the material sphere) and a staff (sovereign power). His youthful appearance is unusual for a king — in the VS deck, royal authority is not always depicted as aged. Parti-colored hose (one leg different from the other) is a fashion of the mid-15th-century Lombardy court, used here to indicate noble rank and fashionable identity. The octagonal throne echoes baptistery architecture — the shape of renewal and sovereignty.

sections.History: A pip card of Cups (Coppe), the suit of Water. Painted in the Bembo workshop for the dukes of Milan, it was a playing card for the game of trionfi — divinatory meanings were attached only centuries later.
sections.Reading: Ace of Cups — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within feeling and relationship — love, devotion, and the inner life.

sections.Scene: A tall elaborate golden fountain-chalice with an eagle finial at the top stands at centre; botanical vines with flowers and foliage fill the card border.
sections.Symbol: The Ace of Cups presents the suit's object in its most exalted form — not a simple cup but a fountain-chalice of extraordinary goldsmith work, topped by the eagle of Visconti heraldry. The chalice form derives from the eucharistic vessel; the ornate fountain type also appears in images of the Garden of Paradise and the Fountain of Life. In Italian tarot, the cups suit is associated with the clergy, the sacramental sphere, and emotional or spiritual life. The eagle finial again asserts dynastic identity over the devotional object.

sections.Symbol: Nine cups in a three-by-three grid fills nearly the entire card face. The density creates a sense of complete saturation — the cup suit at its most abundant. The botanical border is compressed by the number of objects.

sections.Symbol: Ten cups are so numerous they cannot be arranged in a clean grid without overlap — the abundance exceeds orderly composition. This visual density is the culmination of the suit: cups overflowing their frame, touching each other in a suggestion of excess that both completes and slightly disrupts the suit's order.

sections.Scene: A blonde youth in a blue short tunic stands in profile, balancing a small cup on an upturned outstretched palm; gold-tooled background, green grass below.
sections.Symbol: The Page of Cups presents the suit's emblem as an act of offering or balancing — he holds the cup gently, not gripping it. Profile stance and gold background follow Italian court portraiture conventions. The Page of Cups in Italian tradition is associated with emotional receptivity, the first stirrings of feeling, and the service of the devotional or affective sphere.

sections.Scene: A blonde youth on a golden horse rides in profile; he holds a cup or makes a forward gesture; gold-tooled background.
sections.Symbol: The Knight of Cups is the active, mounted face of the suit — movement in service of the emotional or spiritual sphere. The golden horse signals courtly prestige. Unlike the armored Swords Knight, the Cups Knight is lightly dressed, emphasizing grace over force. The forward motion suggests an emissary or messenger of feeling.

sections.Scene: A standing woman in a long dark brocade gown and crown holds a cup adorned with green decoration; gold brocade background; the card shows age-related damage.
sections.Symbol: The Queen of Cups standing rather than enthroned is unusual — she is upright, active, presenting the cup rather than resting with it. The green ornamentation on the cup may be enamel or gemstones, signaling the cup as a precious object of devotion. The VS Queen of Cups is associated with emotional authority exercised through generosity and the offering of the vessel.

sections.History: A court card of Cups (Coppe). The Morgan-Bergamo King of Cups survives but has not been digitized on Wikimedia Commons, so the image shown here is the King of Cups from the Cary-Yale (Visconti di Modrone) deck — the same Bembo workshop, about a decade earlier. In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King; the Cary-Yale deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.

sections.Scene: A bearded man in elaborate red and gold brocade robes sits on an ornate throne holding a large cup; a young page or attendant stands at his right side.
sections.Symbol: The King of Cups is attended by a page — unique among VS courts in showing a two-figure composition. The attendant underscores the king's sovereignty: his cup is served rather than self-held. The elaborate robes are among the most ornate in the VS minor courts. The King of Cups holds authority over the devotional, ceremonial, and emotional sphere — he governs the vessel that all others serve.

sections.Reading: Ace of Swords — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within mind and conflict — thought, strife, decision, and the cut of judgment.

sections.Scene: A single ornate sword with a gold cross-hilt stands vertically at centre; a flowing ribbon banner reading 'awn droyt' spirals around the blue blade; botanical vines with flowers fill the background.
sections.Symbol: The Ace of Swords presents the military and judicial power of the suit in its single, undivided form. The blue blade signals the Visconti heraldic color, while the gold cross-hilt makes the sword a crucifix — connecting military force to Christian authority. The spiraling ribbon with the dynastic motto again claims legitimacy: the sword strikes with good right. The sword as a singular object is both weapon and emblem of justice — in Latin *gladius* serves both functions.

sections.Scene: Two swords cross in an X formation at centre; a ribbon banner reads the Visconti motto; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Two crossed swords introduce the defining visual grammar of the VS swords suit: interlaced blades forming geometric patterns. The X-crossing is also the mark of the Chi-Rho Christogram and of heraldic saltires — a crossing that implies both combat and sacred authority. Two swords in opposition create tension but also balance.

sections.History: The Three of Swords is one of the four cards lost from the Morgan-Bergamo deck. The image shown here is the Three of Swords (Spade) from the Cary-Yale sister deck (c. 1442, same Bembo workshop), where it survives, standing in for the lost original.

sections.Scene: Three swords — two crossed in X plus one upright at centre — form a layered cross shape; a ribbon banner reads the motto; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Three swords with the central upright blade piercing the X of the two crossed ones creates a visual three-way intersection. The three-sword piercing motif recurs in later tarot iconography as a symbol of grief or wounding (it influences the RWS Three of Swords, which shows three swords piercing a heart). In the VS context it remains a geometric arrangement, but the visual logic of penetration and layering is already present.

sections.Scene: Four swords fan outward from the lower centre in a V or upward-radiating pattern; blue lattice stripes with gold diamond lozenges at the intersections; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: From Four onward the VS swords pips shift from crossed pairs with banners to a geometric lattice system: sword blades highlighted with a blue stripe form interlocking diagonals, and gold lozenges mark the intersections. This is pure heraldic geometry — the swords suit rendered as armorial lattice rather than narrative arrangement. The lattice will grow more complex with each higher card. Four swords in a radiating pattern creates the first diamond grid.

sections.Symbol: Five swords expand the lattice further, creating a more complex grid of diagonal intersections. The starburst arrangement pushes swords to the card edges while the lattice interior becomes a heraldic field of lozenges — exactly the kind of pattern used in Visconti heraldic textiles and architectural ornament.

sections.Scene: Six swords in three crossed pairs forming a symmetric geometric lattice; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Six swords make three X-pairs that together create a balanced symmetric lattice. The visual rhythm is one of repeating diamonds formed by the crossing blades. At Six the lattice is mature but not yet complex — there is still visual breathing room between the blades.

sections.Scene: Seven swords in a complex interlaced arrangement; blue lattice stripes with gold-orange lozenges; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Seven swords begin to overwhelm the available space; the lattice becomes denser and the individual sword silhouettes less distinct. The increasingly abstract geometric quality of the higher swords pips suggests the suit's power is expressed through pattern and system rather than individual objects — the military and judicial force of the sword becomes an interlocking web of authority.

sections.Scene: Eight swords form a dense geometric lattice with blue blade stripes and gold-orange diamond fills at all intersections; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Eight swords create a near-complete diamond lattice covering most of the card. The gold-orange fills at intersections add a warmth that counters the cold blue-stripe geometry. Eight is the number of renewal; in the swords suit, eight swords form a web from which, visually, there is no obvious exit — the enclosing power of the suit's force at full extension.

sections.Scene: Nine swords form a very complex dense lattice filling most of the card; gold-orange and green lozenges at intersections; small botanical elements only at the edges.
sections.Symbol: Nine swords compress the card nearly to its borders. The botanical space has shrunk to a thin margin. The lattice is now so dense that individual swords can barely be distinguished — the suit has become an architectural field rather than a collection of weapons. This visual compression anticipates the psychological reading of the Nine of Swords in later traditions as overwhelming mental force.

sections.Scene: Ten swords form the most complex interlaced lattice on the card; gold-green and orange lozenges fill the intersections; a minimal botanical border remains.
sections.Symbol: Ten swords bring the lattice to its extreme — the maximum complexity of the suit's geometric language. The card is almost entirely covered in the interlocking diamond grid, with the botanical frame barely visible. The suit's energy has been entirely abstracted into pattern: this is military and judicial authority as total system, leaving no individual sword distinguishable. The botanical survival at the edge is barely a memory of organic life within the iron lattice.

sections.History: A court card of Swords (Spade). In the four-court Morgan-Bergamo deck the ranks run Page, Knight, Queen, King — the Cary-Yale sister deck unusually doubled these with female knights and damsels.
sections.Reading: Page of Swords — the student and messenger of the suit — its energy young, curious, not yet committed, expressed through mind and conflict — thought, strife, decision, and the cut of judgment.
sections.Iconography: Page of Swords: a courtly fante figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the sword of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.

sections.Scene: A young person in full silver plate armour stands on green grass holding a long sword point-down at the left side; blonde curly hair is visible above the armour collar; gold patterned background.
sections.Symbol: The Page of Swords is unique among VS pages in wearing full plate armour rather than courtly civilian dress. This signals the swords suit's absolute identification with the military sphere: even the youngest figure is fully armed. The sword held point-down is the conventional 'at rest' or 'at ease' posture — readiness without aggression. Plate armour of this quality represents the peak of 15th-century military technology; the Sforza were famous for their armourers.

sections.Reading: Knight of Swords — the suit in motion — questing, active, riding out to meet its element, expressed through mind and conflict — thought, strife, decision, and the cut of judgment.

sections.Scene: A rider in full black plate armour on a dark horse holds a long sword upright; an orange-gold cloak is partially visible; green grass below; gold damask background.
sections.Symbol: The Knight of Swords in full black armour on a dark horse is the most martial image in the VS deck — black armour darkened with heat treatment was a real technology of prestige armour in the 1450s–1460s. The sword held upright signals active authority rather than rest. Black on black (armour on horse) gives this card an intensity unlike the lighter-toned coins and cups knights. The gold damask background frames military power within the luxury of the court.

sections.Scene: A woman in a white gown and gold crown is seated in profile; she holds a long sword upright in a dark gauntleted hand; her other hand is slightly raised.
sections.Symbol: The Queen of Swords in white contrasts sharply with her dark gauntlet — the female figure claims the military attribute directly, not as an allegory. One hand holds the sword; the other, slightly raised, gestures — suggesting judgment pronounced as well as force held. The gauntlet on her sword hand is the only piece of armour she wears, marking the sword as the single instrument of her authority. This is a figure of juridical power: the sword of justice in a woman's grip.

sections.Reading: King of Swords — the outward authority of the suit — it commanded, ordered, and ruled in the world, expressed through mind and conflict — thought, strife, decision, and the cut of judgment.

sections.Scene: A crowned figure in full plate armour sits on an octagonal throne; a long sword is held upright in the right hand; a shield leans at the left; the posture is formal and martial.
sections.Symbol: The King of Swords is the only VS king in full armour — all other suit kings wear courtly robes. His armour identifies the suit's sphere absolutely: the King of Swords rules through force, not ceremony. The shield at his left completes the full panoply of knightly equipment: sword and shield, offense and defense. The octagonal throne grounds his martial power in sovereign legitimacy, suggesting that armed force and just rule are here the same thing — appropriate for Francesco Sforza, a condottiere who became Duke of Milan.

sections.Reading: Ace of Batons — the pure seed and root of the suit, its whole nature held in one, within will and growth — work, enterprise, and the green branch of action.

sections.Scene: A single tall ornate baton with gold bulbous finials and turned wooden sections stands vertically at centre; a flowing ribbon banner reading 'awn droyt' spirals around it; botanical vines fill the background.
sections.Symbol: The Ace of Batons presents the suit's fundamental object with extraordinary artisanal detail: this is not a rough peasant's club but an aristocratized staff with goldsmith-quality finials. The batons suit in Italian tarot is traditionally associated with the peasantry and physical labour — but the VS deck elevates every suit through luxury craftsmanship. The turnery detail (bulbous knobs and spindle shapes) echoes the architectural lathe-turned columns used in Lombard church furnishings.

sections.Scene: Two batons cross in an X formation; a ribbon banner reads the Visconti motto; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Two batons crossed introduce the same compositional vocabulary used in the swords pips — but in the batons suit, the visual grammar will develop differently. Where swords become an abstract lattice of thin blue blades, batons form a more organic lattice of thick knobbly staves. Two batons in X is the simplest crossing, still readable as two distinct objects.

sections.Symbol: Four batons in double-cross (two pairs of X's) create the first complex lattice in the batons suit. The arrangement is more open than the dense higher numbers — individual staves remain clearly legible as objects with distinctive finials.

sections.Scene: Five batons in an arrangement of crossed and fanned staves; ribbon banner reads the motto; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Five batons cannot be resolved into a clean pair-crossing; one baton is always left unpaired. The arrangement typically introduces asymmetry — one baton crossing a pair of pairs, creating the suit's first compositional tension. The ribbon banner does compositional work here, providing a horizontal element that anchors the five-way crossing.

sections.Symbol: Six batons resolve into three X-pairs — a fully symmetric composition. The lattice at six is open enough to still show significant botanical border. The three-X arrangement has a heraldic quality, echoing the saltire patterns used in Lombard armorial design.

sections.Symbol: Seven batons cannot make three even pairs, so one stave crosses differently from the others — the odd number introduces visual asymmetry into the otherwise symmetric lattice. As in the swords suit, higher numbers in the batons pips move from distinct objects toward increasingly abstract geometric fields.

sections.Scene: Eight batons in a symmetric paired lattice; gold-orange diamond lozenges appear at intersections; botanical border.
sections.Symbol: Eight batons in four pairs make the most regular higher lattice in the suit. The diamond lozenges at the intersections — first clearly visible here — are the same heraldic lozenge that appears in the VS textiles, floors, and architectural backgrounds. The batons lattice at eight begins to read as a textile or architectural pattern rather than a collection of objects.

sections.Scene: Nine batons form a complex lattice pattern with gold-orange diamond lozenges at all intersections; small botanical elements at the edges.
sections.Symbol: Nine batons compress the suit's lattice toward the maximum, with the knobbly organic texture of the staves now forming an almost continuous textured field. The orange lozenges at intersections add color warmth to what would otherwise be an entirely brown-and-gold mass. The botanical border has shrunk dramatically.

sections.Scene: Ten batons form the densest lattice, five staves on each side creating a full bilateral arrangement; green and orange lozenges fill the intersections; minimal botanical border remains.
sections.Symbol: Ten batons achieve the maximum expression of the suit's geometric language: two sets of five staves crossing at all possible angles to form a bilateral lattice of extraordinary complexity. The green lozenges at intersections — greener than the orange of earlier cards — introduce a botanical note even within the geometric field, as if the plant world is reasserting itself within the woven structure of the suit. The batons suit at Ten has fully transformed from individual objects into woven architecture.

sections.Scene: A blonde youth in a rich blue cloak with gold-orange trim stands in profile on green grass, holding a tall ornate baton; gold brocade background.
sections.Symbol: The Page of Batons in a courtly blue cloak with orange-gold trim holds the suit emblem as a staff of office rather than a weapon or tool. Unlike the armored Swords Page, the Batons Page is dressed for court service — the baton is ceremonial here. In Italian tarot tradition the batons suit is associated with the active, physical sphere of life, and the Page is its youngest and most open-handed representative. The pendant or finial at the base of the baton is visible — this is a finished, valued object.

sections.Scene: A blonde youth in a blue and orange tunic rides a white horse in motion; the horse appears active or in canter; a baton is held upright; gold damask background.
sections.Symbol: The Knight of Batons on a white horse in motion is the most dynamic of the VS court riders — the horse's active gait gives this card physical energy absent from the more static swords or cups knights. White horse, blue tunic, and orange trim are VS courtly colors. The baton held upright signals active command: the knight of the physical and natural sphere moves energetically through the world.

sections.Scene: A standing woman in a dark blue gown with green brocade sleeves holds a tall baton in her right hand; she stands on an octagonal platform facing forward; gold background.
sections.Symbol: The Queen of Batons standing on an octagonal platform and facing the viewer directly — rather than in profile like several VS queens — gives her unusual frontality and presence. The dark blue gown with green sleeves contrasts richly with the gold background. The green brocade may echo the botanical/vegetative associations of the suit. Her baton is held assertively; she commands the physical sphere through her upright stance and direct gaze.
