sections.Iconography: King of Batons: a courtly re figure of the house of Visconti-Sforza, richly robed in gold brocade and bearing the baton of the suit. The court cards are the deck's portrait gallery — idealised nobles rather than scenes.
sections.Tradition Note: These cards were made to play a game — the Italian *trionfi* / *tarocchi* card game — not for divination, which arrived centuries later. The notes here are historical and iconographic; any reading is a modern interpretive overlay, not part of the deck's original use.
sections.Scene: A crowned man in a dark blue striped robe and gold hose sits on an octagonal gold throne; his legs are crossed casually at the ankles; he holds a short baton in each green-gloved hand.
sections.Symbol: The King of Batons is the most relaxed and informal of the VS kings — his crossed ankles and two-baton pose suggest ease rather than formal authority. The green gloves are a distinctive detail: green in court iconography signals freshness, natural vitality, and the vegetative world — appropriate for the king of the suit associated with physical life and earthly labor. Two batons held simultaneously rather than one is unique; he rules the suit's double nature: structured force (one baton) and natural abundance (the other).

sections.How to Use: Browse the trumps in order to see the medieval social cosmos the deck assumes: craftsman, popess, the imperial and papal powers, love, fortune, the virtues, death, the heavens, and the world entire.
sections.About the Triumphs: The 22 trumps are the tarot's true invention — a fifth suit of allegorical 'triumphs' added to an ordinary 56-card pack around 1440. They were not yet a spiritual journey; they were the high cards of a trick-taking game, a parade of estates from Fool to World. Only in the 18th century did occultists reread them as the soul's progress.
sections.Scene: The Visconti-Sforza trionfi (triumph cards) are the earliest surviving painted tarot trumps of high artistic quality, produced in Milan circa 1450 for the Sforza family. Each is painted in tempera and gold leaf on thick card, with gilded backgrounds and finely modeled figures in courtly dress. The imagery draws on the allegorical tradition of triumphal processions.
sections.Symbol: Gold leaf backgrounds place the figures outside ordinary time and within a sacred or commemorative register. The triumph procession (trionfo) derives from Roman triumphal parades and Petrarch's I Trionfi, making each trump a float in an allegorical march through Fortune, Love, Death, Fame, Time, and Eternity.

sections.How to Use: The suit of having and making — read it for questions of resources, work made tangible, and the slow accumulation of the material world.
sections.About this Suit: Coins (Denari) is the suit of Earth — money, body, land, and provision. In the Morgan-Bergamo deck its pips are gilded discs amid scrolling vines; the Knight of Coins is one of the deck's four lost cards.
sections.Scene: The Coins cards of the Visconti-Sforza show gold disc-shaped coins arranged in geometric patterns against the card surface. The suit pips appear as large, clearly rendered medallion forms. Court cards in the Coins suit show standing or mounted figures bearing shields or banners with coin emblems.
sections.Symbol: The coin disc is a standard symbol of material wealth and earthly value in Italian card traditions. In the Visconti-Sforza context it also carries dynastic resonance: the Sforza arms incorporated a radiant sun disc, and coin imagery aligned with Milanese commercial culture.

sections.How to Use: The suit of the heart — read it for relationship, longing, and the inner life that the trumps dramatise on a cosmic scale.
sections.About this Suit: Cups (Coppe) is the suit of Water — feeling, love, and devotion. Its emblem is the chalice, and in a deck made to celebrate a marriage it carries the deck's romantic charge.
sections.Scene: The Cups cards display ornate chalice or goblet forms as their suit symbol. The cups are rendered as elaborate gold vessels, consistent with the luxury material culture of the Sforza court. Court figures carry or attend to these vessels with ceremonial posture.
sections.Symbol: The chalice is the container of feeling, hospitality, and spiritual content. In Italian court culture the cup also evoked the Eucharistic vessel and the gold plate displayed at aristocratic tables — luxury and devotion overlapping in the same form.

sections.How to Use: The suit of the cutting mind — read it for thought, strife, and the hard clarity of judgment.
sections.About this Suit: Swords (Spade) is the suit of Air — mind, conflict, and decision. Its curved blades interlace across the gold; the Three of Swords is one of the deck's four lost cards.
sections.Scene: The Swords cards show long straight blades arranged in crossed or stacked patterns. The swords are depicted as real weapons of the period — double-edged, with cross-guards — rather than as symbolic abstractions. Court figures in this suit are shown in martial stance.
sections.Symbol: The sword is the symbol of justice, violence, and the ruling class. In Italian tradition the spade suit is associated with the nobility and military. The straight double-edged blade cuts and distinguishes — it is the instrument of discrimination and power.

sections.How to Use: The suit of action and enterprise — read it for ambition, labour, and the green energy of beginnings pushed into the world.
sections.About this Suit: Batons (Bastoni), or Staves, is the suit of Fire — will, work, and growth. It is the deck's only complete minor suit, all fourteen cards surviving.
sections.Scene: The Batons (also called Staves or Bastoni) cards show thick wooden clubs or rod-like implements arranged across the card face. The batons have a rough, organic quality that distinguishes them from the refined metal suits. Some cards show them knotted or gnarled like natural branches.
sections.Symbol: The baton or stave is the implement of labor, authority, and natural force. In Italian tradition the bastoni suit is associated with the merchant class and with the fire element. The rough wooden club stands in contrast to the refined metallic forms of swords and coins.

sections.Why this Matters: The two missing trumps are precisely the deck's images of bondage and catastrophe. A deck made to glorify a dynasty has come down to us with its pictures of the Devil and the falling Tower torn out — a gap that later traditions had to imagine their way across.
sections.About the Lost Cards: Four cards of this deck never survived: The Devil, The Tower, the Knight of Coins, and the Three of Swords. Whether lost to time, accident, or — in the case of the two dark trumps — possibly deliberate removal, we cannot know. Their absence is itself a historical document.
sections.Scene: Six cards of the original Visconti-Sforza deck are missing and were replaced in the eighteenth century by hand-painted facsimiles executed in a different style. The replacement cards — which include The Devil, The Tower, The Star, The Moon, The Sun, and The World — are identifiable by their slightly different scale, color palette, and execution.
sections.Symbol: The replacement cards introduce late-eighteenth-century iconographic conventions into a deck that predates them by three centuries. The visual discontinuity marks the boundary between what the original patrons saw and what later tradition assumed the cards should show.

items.name: The Restoration Hand — Six Repainted Triumphs

sections.Why this Matters: The deck we call 'Visconti-Sforza' is already a restoration — a 15th-century object repaired within its own century. The seams between the two hands are a lesson in how every old deck is partly a reconstruction.
sections.About these Cards: Six trumps — Fortitude, Temperance, the Star, Moon, Sun, and World — were repainted by a later artist (c. 1480s–90s, often attributed to Antonio Cicognara) after the Bembo originals were lost. Their style, faces, and gold differ visibly from the first hand.
sections.Scene: Several cards of the Visconti-Sforza deck show evidence of later intervention — retouching of the gilding, restoration of damaged areas, or overpainting of faces and drapery. These secondary hands are distinguishable from the primary Bembo-workshop execution by differences in line quality and pigment handling.
sections.Symbol: Later restoration work on the deck reflects shifting attitudes toward the objects as collectibles and relics. Each layer of intervention encodes the values of the period that applied it — conservation, display, authentication, or simple repair.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “minor arcana” (40).
sections.Scene: The minor arcana of the Visconti-Sforza consist of fifty-six painted cards divided into four suits. Each suit pip card shows the suit emblem repeated in a plain arrangement on a gilt or plain background. The court cards are richly painted figures against gilded grounds, consistent in quality with the trionfi.
sections.Symbol: The fifty-six minor arcana divide the human world into four elemental registers: fire (batons), water (cups), air (swords), earth (coins). Within each suit the numbered progression from ace to ten maps an arc of unfolding, while the four court figures represent social roles within that element.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “major arcana” (22).
sections.Scene: The major arcana (trionfi) of the Visconti-Sforza are among the oldest surviving tarot trump cards. Painted on thick card with tempera and gold leaf, they depict allegorical and mythological figures in the tradition of the triumph procession. The gold backgrounds give them an iconic, devotional quality distinct from later printed decks.
sections.Symbol: The major arcana encode a cosmological order drawn from Petrarchan allegory, Neoplatonic philosophy, and Christian theology. In the Visconti-Sforza context they also carry a dynastic dimension: the deck was made to honor the Sforza family and the trumps may function as emblems of the virtues they claimed.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “trionfi” (22).
sections.Scene: In the Visconti-Sforza, the trionfi sequence follows an early unnumbered arrangement. The figures — Fool, Magician, Empress, Emperor, Pope, Love, Chariot, Fortitude, Wheel of Fortune, Hermit, Hanged Man, Death, Temperance, Devil, Tower, Star, Moon, Sun, World, Fame, and Time — are depicted as procession floats or courtly allegories.
sections.Symbol: The Italian word trionfi names both the individual trump cards and the allegorical procession tradition they derive from. Petrarch's I Trionfi described six triumphal chariots (Love, Chastity, Death, Fame, Time, Eternity) parading through allegorical space. The tarot trionfi adapt this structure, adding courtly and theological figures to the procession.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “court card” (16).
sections.Scene: Court cards in the Visconti-Sforza show standing figures — knave, knight, queen, and king — against gold-leaf grounds. Each figure wears elaborately detailed clothing with heraldic devices of the Visconti and Sforza families worked into their costume. The figures are frontal or three-quarter, holding their suit attribute.
sections.Symbol: The four court ranks — fante (knave), cavallo (knight), regina (queen), re (king) — represent a condensed feudal hierarchy. Multiplied across four suits, they produce a sixteen-figure matrix that encodes social roles through elemental and hierarchical crossings.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “air” (14).
sections.Scene: The Swords suit in the Visconti-Sforza carries the air quality. The straight, upright blades suggest clarity, intellect, and the cutting edge of discernment.
sections.Symbol: Air is the element of the Swords suit, associated with intellect, communication, conflict, and the swift movement of mind. In Italian-tradition decks the sword cuts through confusion and establishes clarity, but it also wounds.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “batons” (14).
sections.Scene: The Batons or Staves suit carries a fire quality. The wooden rods are rendered as natural, unfinished implements — close to their organic source — suggesting active, generative energy.
sections.Symbol: Batons (bastoni) carry the fire element: active, initiating, creative, and sometimes destructive. The natural wooden rod is unfinished material — energy before it has been shaped into purpose.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “coins” (14).
sections.Scene: The Coins or Denari carry an earth quality. The round, weighty gold discs suggest material substance, value, and the density of worldly resource.
sections.Symbol: Coins (denari) carry the earth element: stable, material, generative, and slow. The disc shape suggests completion and containment — value held and accumulated.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “cups” (14).
sections.Scene: The Cups or Coppe carry a water quality. The chalice forms suggest containment, feeling, and the interior life of emotional experience.
sections.Symbol: Cups (coppe) carry the water element: receptive, emotional, imaginative, and relational. The chalice shape collects and holds — it is the vessel of inner life.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “earth” (14).
sections.Scene: Cards associated with the earth quality in this deck show weighted, stable compositions. Coins are the primary earth suit, with their disc forms suggesting the solidity of matter and material life.
sections.Symbol: Earth is the element of the Coins suit. It grounds the other three elements and gives material form to intention. In the suit sequence earth energy accumulates, consolidates, and endures.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “fire” (14).
sections.Scene: Cards associated with the fire quality show upward movement and active posture. The Batons suit carries this energy through its unrefined wooden implements.
sections.Symbol: Fire is the element of the Batons suit. It initiates, transforms, and consumes. In the suit sequence fire energy moves outward, seeking expression and expansion.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “swords” (14).
sections.Scene: The Swords suit across the Visconti-Sforza deck displays straight blades in formal arrangements. The suit is associated with the air element and with mental and social conflict.
sections.Symbol: Swords carry the air element. The sword is the tool of discernment — it cuts apart to clarify. In the suit sequence air energy discriminates, communicates, and sometimes divides.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “water” (14).
sections.Scene: The Cups suit carries the water quality throughout the Visconti-Sforza. The chalice imagery suggests emotional depth, containment, and the flow of feeling.
sections.Symbol: Water is the element of the Cups suit. It flows, fills, and reflects. In the suit sequence water energy deepens, connects, and sometimes overwhelms.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “bastoni” (10).
sections.Scene: Bastoni is the Italian name for the Batons suit. In the Visconti-Sforza these appear as thick wooden clubs arranged across each pip card. The term bastoni remained standard in Italian-tradition decks.
sections.Symbol: The Italian term bastoni names the Batons suit. Bastoni are the implements of the working and natural world — unrefined wood — and their earthy roughness codes them as the suit closest to natural force and peasant labor in the social hierarchy of Italian court culture.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “coppe” (10).
sections.Scene: Coppe is the Italian name for the Cups suit. In the Visconti-Sforza the coppe are rendered as ornate chalice vessels with elaborate stems and rims, consistent with the goldsmith culture of the Sforza court.
sections.Symbol: Coppe names the Cups suit. In the Italian imaginative tradition the coppe carry associations with hospitality, liturgy, and the interior life. The word coppe also means vaults or hollows — spaces that receive and hold.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “denari” (10).
sections.Scene: Denari (coins) is the Italian name for the Coins suit. The denari in the Visconti-Sforza appear as large flat golden discs, clearly rendered to evoke real currency and material worth.
sections.Symbol: Denari names the Coins suit. The word means money in the most literal sense — the denarius was a Roman coin still in use as a unit of account in medieval Italy. The suit connects material value with the inherited forms of Roman economic life.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “spade” (10).
sections.Scene: Spade is the Italian name for the Swords suit. The spade in the Visconti-Sforza are depicted as real weapons — double-edged and cross-guarded — arranged in stacked or crossed formations on the pip cards.
sections.Symbol: Spade names the Swords suit. The word means swords (and also, in modern Italian, spades in the playing card sense). The straight military blade carried associations with the noble and warrior class in Italian social symbolism.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “ace” (4).
sections.Scene: The Ace cards of each suit in the Visconti-Sforza show a single large suit emblem against a gilt or plain ground, sometimes held by a hand emerging from a cloud — an early example of the hand-from-cloud motif that became standard in later Italian decks.
sections.Symbol: The Ace is the unity from which the suit unfolds. In Italian tradition the Ace sits at the beginning of the pip sequence and concentrates the suit's essential quality in pure, undivided form. The hand-from-cloud motif that sometimes appears on aces signals a gift from an invisible source.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “cavallo” (4).
sections.Scene: Cavallo (the knight on horseback) is one of the four Visconti-Sforza court ranks. The knight card shows a mounted figure in armor carrying the suit attribute on a shield or banner, depicted against a gilded background.
sections.Symbol: The cavallo (knight or horseman) represents the mobile, active energy of the suit. As the second court rank, he sits between the grounded fante and the authoritative figures above. The horse amplifies the suit's elemental quality through movement and natural force.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “eight” (4).
sections.Scene: The Eight pip cards show eight suit emblems arranged in two parallel columns or a loose geometric pattern, suggesting accumulation and density within the suit element.
sections.Symbol: Eight represents the penultimate expansion of the suit before completion. In Neoplatonic number symbolism eight is the number of renewal (7+1), suggesting a return to a higher beginning after the suit's arc has nearly run its course.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “fante” (4).
sections.Scene: Fante (knave or page) is the lowest court rank in the Visconti-Sforza. The fante cards show a standing youthful figure carrying the suit attribute, typically in a tunic and hose against a gilded background.
sections.Symbol: The fante (knave or page) is the lowest court rank — the servant figure who introduces the suit's energy at ground level. In Italian court culture the fante was a household page or foot soldier, giving this card a quality of apprenticeship and beginning service.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “five” (4).
sections.Scene: The Five pip cards show five emblems in an asymmetrical arrangement — two pairs and one isolated element — giving the fives a slightly unbalanced quality compared to the even-numbered cards.
sections.Symbol: Five disrupts the stability of four. It is the number of the human body (five fingers, five senses) and of the pentagram — a figure of dynamic imbalance that opens into new directions. In suit progressions five often names a moment of challenge or loss.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “four” (4).
sections.Scene: The Four pip cards show four emblems in a balanced square or two-by-two arrangement, giving them a stable, symmetrical character.
sections.Symbol: Four is the number of stability, foundation, and structural completion. It names the four elements, four directions, and four seasons. In suit progressions four often signals rest, consolidation, or the static point before new movement begins.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “king” (4).
sections.Scene: The King cards (Re) show a crowned, seated or standing figure of authority in full court dress, bearing the suit attribute and surrounded by heraldic details of the Visconti-Sforza household.
sections.Symbol: The Re (king) is the peak of the court hierarchy. He combines the elemental quality of his suit with the fullest expression of social authority. In the Visconti-Sforza the king figures carry Sforza heraldry, linking dynastic power directly to the elemental suits.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “knight” (4).
sections.Scene: The Knight cards (Cavallo) show a mounted figure in armor, the most dynamic of the court figures. The horse and rider together convey movement and martial energy.
sections.Symbol: The Cavallo (knight) embodies the suit's energy in active motion. Mounted on horseback, he occupies the social register between the grounded fante and the enthroned courts — the figure of questing, errantry, and pursuit.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “nine” (4).
sections.Scene: The Nine pip cards show nine emblems arranged in three rows of three, giving them a sense of abundance and near-completion within the suit.
sections.Symbol: Nine is the number of fulfillment approaching completion. In Pythagorean tradition nine is the last single digit and contains all numbers within itself. In suit progressions nine often names a peak of the suit's quality before the culmination at ten.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “page” (4).
sections.Scene: Page cards (Fante) show standing youthful figures, the attendants of the court. They hold their suit attribute forward, as if presenting or offering it.
sections.Symbol: The Page (fante) introduces each suit at its most receptive and unformed state. As the servant of the court, the page carries the suit's energy without yet channeling it through authority. The page is the pupil, the messenger, the one who begins.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “queen” (4).
sections.Scene: The Queen cards (Regina) show a seated or standing female figure in elaborate court dress with a crown or headdress, holding or attended by the suit attribute.
sections.Symbol: The Regina (queen) holds the interior authority of the suit — the nourishing, organizing, or protective expression of the element. In court hierarchies she is the counterpart to the king's external power, embodying an inward or generative aspect of the same elemental energy.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “re” (4).
sections.Scene: Re (king) is the highest court rank in Italian-tradition decks. The Re cards of the Visconti-Sforza show crowned figures in full majesty, often with heraldic devices of the Sforza family incorporated into their robes.
sections.Symbol: The Re (king) is the peak of the court hierarchy. He combines the elemental quality of his suit with the fullest expression of social authority. In the Visconti-Sforza the king figures carry Sforza heraldry, linking dynastic power directly to the elemental suits.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “regina” (4).
sections.Scene: Regina (queen) is the female court rank in the Visconti-Sforza. The regina cards show crowned or veiled female figures in rich brocade dress, holding the suit attribute with a formal, composed bearing.
sections.Symbol: The Regina (queen) expresses the receptive or nurturing face of the suit's authority. In Italian court culture the queen figure often held spiritual or patronal roles, and the Visconti-Sforza regina cards reflect the high social status of the women for whom the deck was likely made.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “seven” (4).
sections.Scene: The Seven pip cards show seven emblems in an irregular arrangement, carrying a sense of movement or incompleteness that distinguishes them from the stable sixes.
sections.Symbol: Seven is the number of cycles and spiritual development. The seven days, seven planets, and seven classical virtues all run through this number. In suit progressions seven often names a moment of testing or vision within the suit's arc.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “six” (4).
sections.Scene: The Six pip cards arrange six emblems in two parallel columns of three, creating a balanced and visually resolved composition.
sections.Symbol: Six is the number of harmony and reciprocity. It is the first perfect number (1+2+3=6). In suit progressions six often names a moment of balance, gift, or resolution following the disruption of five.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “ten” (4).
sections.Scene: The Ten pip cards show ten suit emblems filling the card space, giving the impression of abundance and culmination at the end of the numbered sequence.
sections.Symbol: Ten names completion and the return to unity at a higher level. It is 1+0 — the one reborn after the full cycle of the single digits. In suit progressions ten represents the suit's full expression and the threshold before the next beginning.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “three” (4).
sections.Scene: The Three pip cards show three emblems in a triangular arrangement, suggesting initial expansion and the first dynamic grouping beyond the pair.
sections.Symbol: Three is the number of dynamic expansion beyond duality. It introduces a third term that transforms a pair into a field. In suit progressions three often names initial growth, collaboration, or creative output.

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “two” (4).
sections.Scene: The Two pip cards show a pair of suit emblems, often mirrored or balanced, suggesting duality, exchange, and the beginning of relationship within the suit.
sections.Symbol: Two introduces duality, exchange, and relationship into the suit. It is the first difference from the Ace's unity. In suit progressions two often names a choice, a pairing, or the beginning of negotiation.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
